title: Canada’s pipeline plans | Heatwaves hit 4 July events | Côte d'Ivoire’s deadly floods - Carbon Brief
gist: Carbon Brief's daily digest bundles Canada's plan for a 1m-barrel/day Pacific oil pipeline to cut US dependence, a record US north-east heatwave straining grids and datacentres, 2,000+ European heat deaths, deadly West African floods, China warming faster than the global average, and Exxon's stock-driven comeback under Darren Woods.

themes.theme: Canada's Pacific pipeline and energy-superpower pivot

insights.claim: Canadian PM Mark Carney announced plans for a new oil pipeline of more than 1,000km from Alberta to the coast of British Columbia to supply Asia with up to 1m barrels of oil a day, explicitly to break dependence on selling to the US market.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A new Pacific export route redirects Canadian crude toward Asian buyers, a structural shift in North American oil flows worth watching for pipeline builders, tanker demand and US refiner sourcing.
insights.quote: seeks to make the nation an ‘energy superpower’ and break its dependence on [selling to] the US market
insights.locator: Canada unveils plans for new oil pipeline

insights.claim: The proposed west-coast Canadian pipeline is expected to cost C$35-44bn and be completed around 2032-34, with Alberta targeting September 2027 for project submission approval.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A multi-decade, C$35-44bn capex project signals long-dated infrastructure spend and execution risk; the 2032-34 timeline means near-term export relief is limited.
insights.locator: Canada unveils plans for new oil pipeline

insights.claim: Canada plans to more than triple its LNG production by developing five terminals over the next decade, spend C$10bn upgrading the Vancouver port, and has set a goal to double its non-US exports within a decade.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A coordinated push to triple LNG and double non-US exports reframes Canada as a major Pacific-facing hydrocarbon supplier, relevant to LNG offtakers and Asian import demand.
insights.locator: Canada unveils plans for new oil pipeline

themes.theme: US north-east heatwave, grids and datacentre strain

insights.claim: Record-breaking heat pushed New York and Boston to 100F and prompted widespread cancellation of 4 July fireworks, concerts and parades across the US north-east, with the heat index forecast to reach 115F in Washington DC, Philadelphia and New York.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Extreme-heat disruption of major public events and outdoor World Cup matches signals rising physical climate risk to US infrastructure and event economics.
insights.locator: North-east US heatwave causes 4 July cancellations

insights.claim: Hot weather drives up electricity demand for datacentres exactly when the grid has least capacity to give, stressing datacentres and the power grid simultaneously and worsening local air quality.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Correlated heat-driven grid stress and AI datacentre demand peaks is a structural bottleneck for the AI buildout; watch power availability, backup generation and grid siting as constraints on compute expansion.
insights.quote: Datacentres need the most energy exactly when the grid has the least available to give.
insights.locator: several publications report on how datacentres are adding strain

insights.claim: The Trump administration urged datacentres to run their back-up generators during the heatwave as electricity prices soared and many thousands lost power.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Reliance on diesel back-up generation for datacentres during grid stress reveals reliability gaps and adds emissions/air-quality liabilities to AI-infrastructure economics.
insights.locator: several publications report on how datacentres are adding strain

insights.claim: A rapid attribution analysis by the World Weather Attribution group found the current combined heat and humidity would be virtually impossible without fossil-fuelled climate change.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Formal attribution linking specific extreme events to fossil emissions strengthens the case for climate litigation and physical-risk repricing.
insights.locator: CNN reports on a rapid analysis

themes.theme: European heat mortality and political fallout

insights.claim: June's record heat caused more than 2,000 excess deaths across Spain and France, with France reporting around 1,000 excess deaths over 24-28 June (likely an underestimate) and the Netherlands reporting 480 from the same period.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Quantified heat mortality across major EU economies underscores mounting adaptation costs and public-health liabilities from recurring heatwaves.
insights.locator: Europe: Spain and France face more heat

insights.claim: France's government is to face a no-confidence vote over its handling of the heatwave, while temperatures in south-east Spain could hit 42-44C.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Heatwave management becoming a trigger for a no-confidence vote shows climate adaptation failures translating directly into political and sovereign-stability risk.
insights.locator: Europe: Spain and France face more heat

themes.theme: West Africa floods and adaptation gap

insights.claim: At least 59 people have been killed in Côte d'Ivoire floods since May amid relentless rains across coastal west Africa that authorities predict could intensify further.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Escalating flood mortality in a low-emitting region highlights the adaptation-finance gap and loss-and-damage exposure for vulnerable economies.
insights.locator: Côte d'Ivoire floods kill 59

insights.claim: Despite Africa contributing only minimally to greenhouse-gas emissions, the World Meteorological Organization says the continent is especially vulnerable to extreme weather events made deadlier by climate breakdown.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: The emissions-vulnerability mismatch is the core equity argument driving climate-finance and loss-and-damage negotiations that shape aid and MDB flows.
insights.locator: Côte d'Ivoire floods kill 59

themes.theme: China warming, green-tech and policy hardening

insights.claim: A China Meteorological Administration report finds China is warming faster than the global average, with annual average temperature rising 0.31C per decade from 1961 to 2025 and extreme high-temperature events increasing significantly since the early 21st century.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Faster-than-average warming in the world's largest emitter raises domestic adaptation urgency and could reshape China's cooling-demand and energy-policy trajectory.
insights.locator: China warming faster than global average

insights.claim: Chinese manufacturers are moving to fully ensure supply of cooling products to the European market, with Chinese state media arguing that European 'threat', 'trade deficit' and 'overcapacity' framings look increasingly hollow amid Europe's heatwaves.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Heat-driven European demand for Chinese cooling and green tech complicates EU protectionist narratives; watch for trade-policy tension around Chinese climate-tech imports.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, Chinese manufacturers

insights.claim: China's new renewable-energy-consumption policy has shifted the industry from 'soft guidance' to a 'hard constraint', with carbon-emission constraints to be embedded across industry, investment and consumption over the next five years.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A move from voluntary to binding renewable/carbon constraints signals firmer Chinese decarbonisation policy, bullish for domestic clean-energy deployment and demand.
insights.locator: MORE ON CHINA

insights.claim: Inner Mongolia in northern China is adding both new clean-energy and new coal capacity simultaneously, with long-term power contracts remaining a key obstacle to reducing coal generation.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Long-term power contracts locking in coal even as renewables grow is a structural brake on China's coal phase-down, relevant to coal-demand and emissions forecasts.
insights.locator: MORE ON CHINA

insights.claim: The World Bank will phase out its lending to China by 2031.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: The end of World Bank lending to China reflects its graduation from concessional finance and redirects multilateral capacity toward lower-income borrowers.
insights.locator: MORE ON CHINA

insights.claim: Over the past five years Exxon shares surged 115%, more than any other US or European oil major, and it holds the largest proven oil and gas reserves among publicly traded western majors while leading on return on capital employed.
insights.speaker: Jamie Smyth and Kenza Bryan, Financial Times
insights.so_what: Exxon's outperformance and reserve/ROCE leadership make it the benchmark for capital discipline among oil majors, relevant to energy-sector allocation.
insights.quote: Over the past five years Exxon shares have surged 115%, more than any other US or European oil major.
insights.locator: The king of big oil: Exxon's comeback

insights.claim: Exxon CEO Darren Woods accepts human activity is a major cause of climate change and urged Trump to keep the US in the Paris agreement, yet has leveraged his proximity to the Trump administration to become a fierce opponent of climate legislation, especially in Europe.
insights.speaker: Jamie Smyth and Kenza Bryan, Financial Times
insights.so_what: A CEO who accepts climate science while fighting climate legislation reveals a more sophisticated, harder-to-counter oil-major strategy that could stall EU regulation.
insights.quote: Woods has leveraged his proximity to the Trump administration to become a fierce opponent of climate legislation, especially in Europe.
insights.locator: The king of big oil: Exxon's comeback

insights.claim: Guardian environment editor Adam Morton argues Trump may have inadvertently exposed the vulnerability of fossil fuels through his war on Iran.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A framing that geopolitical oil shocks expose rather than reinforce fossil-fuel strength is a contrarian angle on energy-security narratives.
insights.locator: MORE COMMENT

themes.theme: Research signals and other data points

insights.claim: Regional ocean dynamics regulate the efficiency of ocean alkalinity enhancement as a carbon-dioxide-removal method, meaning CDR effectiveness is location-dependent rather than uniform.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Location-dependent CDR efficiency matters for siting and cost of ocean-based carbon-removal ventures and the credibility of associated carbon credits.
insights.locator: Regional ocean dynamics regulate

insights.claim: US natural gas is set to surpass oil as the country's top energy source by 2030, and India's June coal-fired power output rose to its highest since November 2023.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Gas overtaking oil in the US and rising Indian coal output show fossil demand shifting rather than collapsing, relevant to long-term commodity-mix bets.
insights.locator: Other Stories

insights.claim: Tesla deliveries climbed 25% as a fuel-price surge boosted European demand.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Fuel-price-driven EV demand shows high oil prices acting as a tailwind for EV makers, a hedge relationship worth tracking against oil-major strength.
insights.locator: Other Stories
